medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Stimulates alpha and beta adrenergic receptors. Alpha: vasoconstriction (increases BP). Beta-1: increases heart rate and contractility. Beta-2: bronchodilation.
medication_cards.dose_adult: 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1,000 concentration) for anaphylaxis. Auto-injector: 0.3 mg adult.
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1,000). Auto-injector: 0.15 mg for <30 kg.
medication_cards.special_considerations: May repeat every 5-15 minutes as needed. Monitor for rebound anaphylaxis. Keep patient supine with legs elevated if hypotensive.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Selectively stimulates beta-2 adrenergic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchodilation and reduced airway resistance.
contraindications: Use caution with tachycardia or cardiac history
medication_cards.dose_adult: 2.5 mg in 3 mL NS via nebulizer
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: 2.5 mg in 3 mL NS via nebulizer (same dose)
medication_cards.special_considerations: Can repeat as needed per protocol. Monitor heart rate. May be given with ipratropium. Nebulizer requires O2 at 6-8 LPM to generate mist.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX), preventing formation of thromboxane A2, which reduces platelet aggregation and clot formation.
indications: Chest pain of suspected cardiac origin
contraindications: Children with viral illness (Reye syndrome risk)
medication_cards.dose_adult: 324 mg (4 baby aspirin) chewed, not swallowed whole
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: Not indicated in pediatric cardiac emergencies at AEMT level
medication_cards.special_considerations: Patient must be alert enough to swallow. Ask about allergies before administration. Chewing speeds absorption. Give early in suspected ACS — do not wait for hospital.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Relaxes vascular smooth muscle, primarily venous dilation. Reduces preload, decreases myocardial oxygen demand, and dilates coronary arteries.
indications: Chest pain of suspected cardiac origin
contraindications: Use of phosphodiesterase inhibitors (Viagra/Cialis/Levitra) within 24-72 hours
medication_cards.dose_adult: 0.4 mg sublingual tablet or spray. May repeat every 3-5 minutes up to 3 doses (per protocol). Recheck BP before each dose.
medication_cards.special_considerations: MUST check BP before EVERY dose. Hypotension is the primary concern. Have IV access established before or immediately after administration. Patient should be sitting or supine. Highly testable contraindications on NREMT.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Provides readily absorbable sugar to raise blood glucose levels. Absorbed through GI tract.
indications: Hypoglycemia (BGL < 60 mg/dL) in a patient who is alert and able to swallow
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: Same — 15-25 g if able to swallow
side_effects: Aspiration risk if patient loses consciousness
medication_cards.special_considerations: Patient MUST be alert enough to swallow and protect airway. Place between cheek and gum. Recheck BGL 15 minutes after administration. If patient cannot take PO, consider glucagon (if in protocol) or IV dextrose.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Competitively binds to opioid receptors, reversing the effects of opioids including respiratory depression, sedation, and hypotension.
indications: Respiratory depression with suspected opioid use
medication_cards.dose_adult: 2 mg IN (intranasal via MAD) or 0.4-2 mg IV/IM. May repeat every 2-3 minutes as needed.
side_effects: Acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, tachycardia)
medication_cards.special_considerations: Duration of naloxone is SHORTER than most opioids — monitor for re-narcotization. Patient may become combative upon awakening. Have restraint plan and scene safety awareness. Titrate to adequate respirations, not full consciousness. IN route preferred in field (no sharps risk).

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Expands intravascular volume. Isotonic — does not shift fluid between intracellular and extracellular compartments. Replaces fluid losses and maintains vascular volume.
contraindications: CHF (use caution — risk of fluid overload)
medication_cards.dose_adult: Bolus: 500-1000 mL (20 mL/kg) IV wide open for shock. KVO: 30-60 mL/hr for access maintenance.
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: 20 mL/kg bolus, may repeat up to 60 mL/kg
medication_cards.duration: Variable — approximately 1/3 remains intravascular after 1 hour
medication_cards.special_considerations: Most common AEMT fluid. Monitor for signs of fluid overload (crackles, JVD, peripheral edema) during bolus. Warm fluids if hypothermic patient. Drip rate calculation: gtts/min = (Volume x Drop factor) / Time in minutes.

medication_cards.mechanism_of_action: Blocks serotonin (5-HT3) receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and vagal nerve terminals, preventing nausea and vomiting.
contraindications: Caution in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities (prolonged QT)
medication_cards.dose_adult: 4 mg IV (slow push over 2-5 min) or 4 mg ODT (orally disintegrating tablet) SL
medication_cards.dose_pediatric: 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) or 4 mg ODT for >40 kg
medication_cards.special_considerations: ODT dissolves on tongue — good option when IV not available or patient is vomiting. IV push must be slow (2-5 minutes) to avoid arrhythmia risk. Check if in SNHD AEMT protocol.

checkpoint_config.description: Pharm checkpoint cards present medication information with key fields blanked out. Students must fill in the missing information from memory.

level_2.description: Medium — adds MOA, contraindications, side effects

level_3.description: Hard — most fields blanked, near-complete recall
